title: Product Design Using a Generative Adversarial Network: Incorporating Consumer Preferences and External Data
study_design_description: The study applies a generative adversarial network framework to product design in an empirical context with a self-operated photography chain. The framework's efficacy is demonstrated through an empirical application where the model generated superior photo template designs. Web-based experiments were conducted to verify the method and confirm its effectiveness across varying design contexts. Direct consumer evaluation on the generated designs was obtained through an online survey on Credamo, a leading survey platform in China.
primary_task_description: In the survey, respondents imagine entering a self-service photo booth to take personal photos. They are presented with 8 templates (similar number of templates per theme are presented in the real business setting) and asked to choose the 2 most suitable backgrounds. The 8 templates include: 2 from CcGAN with internal and external preferences (randomly from A1-A4), 2 from CcGAN with internal preference (randomly from B1-B4), 2 from DCGAN (randomly selected from C1-C4), and 2 internal original templates (D1-D2).
population_description: The internal dataset obtained from the company contains the original template images that the company offered to customers, without the presence of consumers inside the images. The company offered 83 photo themes in total during the observation period. Each theme contains 1 to 13 templates, yielding a total of 585 templates across all the themes. The second part of the internal dataset contains the complete order histories of 2195 consumers who had taken at least one photo between October 2017 and August 2018. The focus is on photo samples from the category "Main (single)," which includes 931 consumers and 33,804 consumer-template pairs. Out of these 33,804 pairs, 3,629 are positive samples, and 30,175 are negative samples. The final sample for the survey includes 288 respondents.

arms.description: A deep convolutional GAN (DCGAN) that generates new designs from internal and external existing designs in UGC without incorporating consumer preferences.
CcGAN version using only internal consumer preferences for training the predictor.
CcGAN version utilizing both internal and external preferences, representing the final framework version.
Internal original templates offered by the company.

outcomes.outcome_description: A distance-based metric employed to assess the similarity of the generated templates to popular existing ones. The distance-based metric for each generative model is defined as D = 1/N ∑[∑(d²ij) + ∑(1 − dik)²], where N denotes the total number of templates generated by each generative model, J denotes the set of the most popular internal and external templates, and K denotes the set of the least popular internal and external templates.
Model-level choice probability is calculated as the number of times templates from a specific model are chosen divided by the number of times they are shown.
Template-level choice probability is calculated as the number of times a template is chosen divided by the number of times it is shown.

A personalized distance-based metric calculated by calculating the distance of each generated template to a set of target images that represent the target consumer's most- and least-preferred existing templates.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: internal_original_templates vs dcgan vs ccgan_internal_preference vs ccgan_internal_external_preferences
reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: CcGAN with internal and external preferences shows 9.13% improvement over internal original images
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Table 2 presents the mean and median distance-based metrics for each set of images. Internal original images: Mean 1.32, Median 1.38. DCGAN: Mean 1.26, Median 1.25. CcGAN with internal preference: Mean 1.25, Median 1.22. CcGAN with internal and external preferences (no label weights): Mean 1.18, Median 1.19. CcGAN with internal and external preferences: Mean 1.14, Median 1.13.

CcGAN with internal and external preferences shows the largest performance improvement over the internal original images (9.13%) because Prediction Model 2 allows it to fully incorporate popular features from both internal and external sources.

CcGAN with internal preference only shows a limited performance improvement (2%) as Prediction Model 1 is trained solely on internal preferences and cannot fully recognize out-of-the-box features from external templates.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: ccgan_internal_external_preferences vs ccgan_internal_preference vs dcgan vs internal_original_templates
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Table 3 presents the template-level and model-level choice probabilities from the survey data. CcGAN with internal and external preferences: Model-level 33.0%. CcGAN with internal preference: Model-level 23.3%. DCGAN: Model-level 16.0%. Internal original images: Model-level 27.8%.
CcGAN with internal and external preferences: A1: 24.7%, A2: 45.2%, A3: 36.0%, A4: 26.1%.
CcGAN with internal preference: B1: 23.5%, B2: 22.7%, B3: 23.7%, B4: 23.1%.

DCGAN does not perform well when evaluated by Prediction Model 1. Although DCGAN incorporates new design features from external templates, Prediction Model 1 cannot recognize the attractiveness of these new features and treats them as unpopular. Thus, DCGAN-generated templates perform worse than the original internal images (-1.61%).
DCGAN performs better (4.47%) when evaluated by Prediction Model 2, as this model can recognize popular features from external sources in the DCGAN-generated templates.

Table 5 presents the means and medians of the personalized distance-based metrics by the personalized and non-personalized models. DCGAN: Mean 1.22, Median 1.22. CcGAN with internal and external preferences: Mean 1.10, Median 1.05. CcGAN with internal and external preferences (personalized): Mean 0.97, Median 0.96.

notes: This is an empirical study applying a novel framework for product design using generative adversarial networks (GANs) to a real business setting (a self-operated photography chain in China). The paper compares three model variants: DCGAN (baseline without consumer preferences), CcGAN with internal preferences only, and CcGAN with both internal and external preferences. Performance is evaluated through three methods: distance-based metrics, online consumer survey (n=288), and prediction model-based choice probabilities. The paper also includes a personalized design generation exercise. The study demonstrates that incorporating both internal and external consumer preferences into the generative model produces superior designs compared to models without preference information. No p-values or statistical significance tests are reported in the provided quotes. The internal dataset contains 2195 consumers and 33,804 consumer-template pairs (931 consumers in the "Main (single)" category). The survey sample includes 288 respondents. The paper notes several limitations: the enhanced model (CcGAN with internal preference only) is trained only on internal consumer choices and lacks exposure to diverse design styles from external data; external consumers are used in training Prediction Model 2 but not in the prediction stage; the training set for the GAN is relatively small (1321 images after exclusions); and the sample is unbalanced with positive samples accounting for only 10.7% of the total.
